definition: To bewitch is to work witchcraft upon someone — to lay a spell that binds, sickens, or entrances, often against the victim's will. Unlike a charm that merely delights, bewitching is charged with agency and harm: a cow struck barren, a king made pliant, a lover ensnared. It names the effect from the sufferer's side, the state of being under another's craft.
history: The word is Old English *wiccian*, 'to work magic,' with the intensifying prefix *be-*: to witch someone, harder. Its medieval life ran on *maleficium*, the language of harm — Kieckhefer notes the commonest charge in the courts was that an old woman had 'bewitched his cows or his children.' The turn is a softening: the diplomat Chapuys claimed Anne Boleyn had so 'enchanted and bewitched' Henry VIII he would deny her nothing, and by then the word could mean fascination as easily as injury. So a term of injury became one of allure — and, in Magic, *Bewitch* is a keyword that binds an Aura's fate to an enchanted creature.

def_sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages (bewitched cows and children)

etymology.origin: Old English be- (intensive) + wiccian 'to work magic'
etymology.note: To 'witch', intensified. The fashion sense — 'to bewitch a room' — is a softened spell; now also an MTG keyword.

cards.name: Bewitching Leechcraft
cards.set_name: The Lord of the Rings: Tales of Middle-earth

flavor_snippets.html: A gwyllion's favorite trap is the vanity mirror. A <mark>bewitched</mark> piece of glass traps the looker's soul and does away with the body.
The stronger the will, the more <mark>bewitching</mark> the song.

mechanics_snippets.html:
<mark>Bewitching</mark> Whispers — When Shessra, Death's Whisper enters, target creature blocks this turn if able.
Whispers of the Grave — At the beginning of your end step, if a creature died this turn, you may pay 2 life. If you do, draw a card.
